era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is consolidating in Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman I is at its height; the Ming Dynasty rules China; the Mughal Empire under Humayun is expanding in South Asia. European exploration of the Americas is intensifying, with competing colonial claims. The Catholic Church faces institutional crisis and reform movements.

events.title: Act of Supremacy (England)
events.description: Parliament passes the Act of Supremacy, establishing Henry VIII as Supreme Head of the Church of England, severing the English church from papal authority. This formalizes the break initiated by Henry's marriage to Anne Boleyn and rejection of papal authority over the annulment of his first marriage. The act marks a watershed in English religious and political history, subordinating ecclesiastical power to royal prerogative and enabling the subsequent dissolution of the monasteries.

sources.name: Letters and Papers, Foreign and Domestic, of the Reign of Henry VIII

events.certainty_note: Exact date is 3 November 1534. Parliamentary records are contemporary and unambiguous.

events.title: Founding of the Society of Jesus (Jesuits)
events.description: Ignatius of Loyola and six companions take vows in Montmartre, Paris, formally establishing the Society of Jesus (Societas Iesu). Approved by Pope Paul III in 1540, the Jesuits become the vanguard of the Catholic Counter-Reformation, emphasizing education, missionary work, and absolute obedience to papal authority. The order will become one of the most influential religious institutions in early modern history, establishing schools and missions across Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

sources.name: Ignatius of Loyola, Spiritual Exercises (written 1522–1541, compiled later)
Vita Ignatii Loyolae by Juan Alfonso de Polanco (Jesuit chronicle, 16th century)

events.certainty_note: The vows were taken on 15 August 1534 in Paris. Papal approval came in 1540. The founding moment is well-documented in Jesuit sources.
cross_references: 1540-001 (Papal approval of the Society of Jesus)

events.title: Jacques Cartier's Second Voyage to North America
events.region: North America (St. Lawrence River region, modern Canada)
events.description: French explorer Jacques Cartier undertakes his second voyage to North America, sailing up the St. Lawrence River and establishing contact with Iroquoian peoples. He winters at Stadacona (modern Quebec City) and explores further inland, claiming the territory for France. The voyage yields detailed observations of indigenous peoples and geography, strengthening French claims to North American territory and laying groundwork for future French colonial expansion in Canada.

sources.name: Cartier, Jacques. Relation originale du voyage de Jacques Cartier au Canada en 1534 (Primary account, published 1598)

events.certainty_note: Cartier's voyage is dated 1534–1535. Contemporary accounts and later compilations confirm the dates and major events.

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid War: Siege of Baghdad
events.description: Ottoman forces under Süleyman I besiege and capture Baghdad from Safavid control, establishing Ottoman dominance over Mesopotamia. The siege lasts from November 1534 to April 1535. The conquest consolidates Ottoman power in the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East, shifting the balance of power decisively against the Safavid Persian Empire and establishing Ottoman control over key trade routes and religious sites (including the Shia shrine cities).

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles (Peçevi, Solakzade, 17th-century compilations of earlier records)

events.certainty_note: The siege is dated November 1534–April 1535 in Ottoman and Safavid sources. The outcome is unambiguous.

events.title: Anabaptist Kingdom of Münster (Radical Reformation)
events.description: Radical Anabaptist preachers, including Jan van Leiden and Jan Beukelsz, seize control of the city of Münster in Westphalia, establishing a theocratic commune based on radical Christian principles including communal property and polygamy. The movement attracts thousands of followers and provokes fierce opposition from Catholic and Lutheran authorities. The kingdom lasts until 1535, when it is besieged and destroyed, but it becomes a defining moment in the Radical Reformation and a cautionary tale for both Protestant and Catholic establishments.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by Catholic and Lutheran observers (e.g., Heinrich Gresbeck, eyewitness account)

events.certainty_note: The takeover occurred in early 1534. Contemporary accounts from both supporters and opponents confirm the basic facts, though interpretations vary.

events.title: Mughal Conquest of Gujarat
events.description: Mughal Emperor Humayun, with assistance from his brother Askari, conquers the sultanate of Gujarat from the Gujarati Sultan Bahadur Shah. The conquest expands Mughal territory westward and consolidates control over western India. However, Humayun's hold on Gujarat proves temporary; Bahadur Shah recovers the territory in 1536, and Mughal control remains contested until later campaigns.

sources.name: Abul Fazl, Akbar Nama (16th-century Mughal chronicle, written after Humayun's reign)
Firishta, Gulshan-i Ibrahim (17th-century Persian history of India)

events.certainty_note: Mughal sources date the conquest to 1534, but the chronology of Humayun's early campaigns is somewhat uncertain. The conquest was temporary, which complicates its historical significance.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Humayun's Gujarat conquest is often overstated in Mughal historiography. While presented as a major victory, the conquest was short-lived and Bahadur Shah recovered the territory within two years, suggesting Humayun's military position was weaker than court chronicles imply. Additionally, some modern scholars question whether the Anabaptist Kingdom of Münster should be characterized as a 'kingdom' at all, given its brief duration and limited territorial control; it may be better understood as a radical urban commune that was quickly suppressed.
historiographic_note: The year 1534 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly for England and the Reformation. Ottoman and Safavid sources provide good coverage of the Baghdad campaign, though with expected bias toward their respective sides. Indigenous American perspectives on Cartier's voyage are absent from surviving records; knowledge of this encounter comes entirely from European sources. Mughal sources are less contemporary and more hagiographic regarding Humayun's early campaigns. The record is heavily weighted toward political and religious events; economic and social history are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The Act of Supremacy enabled the Dissolution of the Monasteries
The Jesuit vows of 1534 led to papal approval and formal establishment in 1540
Cartier's second voyage led directly to his third voyage in 1535
The Baghdad siege was part of the broader Ottoman-Safavid conflict resolved by the Treaty of Amasya
The Anabaptist takeover of Münster led directly to its siege and destruction in 1535
